title: 117 years of French rugby on a map
Mapping the 2023 rugby World Cup
text:
With the 2023 Rugby World Cup that just started, I decided to combine two passions of mine: geography and rugby - with a bit of a computational twist.
Sourcing data from this Wikipedia page, I collected the list of all players participating in the world cup, representing a total of 20 teams. Replicating what I did for the French team in a previous post, I extracted - when available - the place and country of birth.
The result is this simple interactive website, showing how deeply transnational the making of international teams is now, since rugby became a professional sport in the late 1990s.
You can explore the composition of each team or identify which country is the most represented across the 20 teams.
The script and the data can be accessed in this repository.

title: Julien Migozzi
text:
ENGLISH FRENCH


Recherche
A la croisée de la géographie, des études urbaines et de la sociologie économique, mes recherches portent sur les technologies numériques, la finance, et les villes, afin de comprendre comment le capitalisme numérique affecte le fonctionnement des marchés et l’évolution des inégalités socio-spatiales. Je mobilise des méthodes mixtes, associant l’enquête ethnographique à l’analyse spatiale et aux sciences sociales computationnelles.
Mon premier axe de recherche analyse comment le capitalisme numérique transforme les marchés immobiliers et influence les processus de stratification sociale et de ségrégation urbaine. J’étudie notamment l’industrie de la PropTech (PropertyTechnology), soit l’ensemble des technologiques numériques appliqué au secteur de l’immobilier.
La géographie de la finance constitue mon deuxième champ de recherche. J’étudie nnotamment rôle du numérique (FinTech) et du capital-risque (private equity) dans la formation des écosystemes et marchés financiers aux échelles internationales, régionales et urbaines.
L’Afrique du Sud et la ville du Cap, où je mène des recherches approfondies depuis 2015, constituent mon principal cas d’étude et terrain d’enquête.
Je suis co-auteur de l’Atlas de la Finance, publié aux Presses Universitaires de Yale et traduit en français aux Éditions du Seuil.


Parcours
Je suis ancien élève de l’École Normale Supérieure de Lyon et agrégé de géographie. J’ai réalisé mon doctorat à l’Université Grenoble Alpes et à l’UMR Géographie-cités (Paris). Ma thèse a reçu deux prix nationaux : le Grand Prix de Thése sur la ville et le Prix de Thèse du Comité National Français de Géographie.
A l’University d’Oxford, j’ai été lauréat de la Postdoctoral Research Fellowship décernée par la Urban Studies Foundation (2022-2026), et postdoctorant en Finance et Géographie sur le projet ERC Cities in Global Financial Networks (2021-2022). Auparavant, j’ai occupé les fonctions d’Agrégé-Préparateur et d’ATER au Département de Géographie de l’École Normale Supérieure (2017-2021). J’ai été chercheur invité à l’African Centre for Cities (Université du Cap, 2016 et 2023) ainsi qu’au Département de Sociologie de UC Berkeley (2024-2025).

title: Mapping the 2023 rugby World Cup
text:
With the 2023 Rugby World Cup that just started, I decided to combine two passions of mine: geography and rugby - with a bit of a computational twist.
Sourcing data from this Wikipedia page, I collected the list of all players participating in the world cup, representing a total of 20 teams. Replicating what I did for the French team in a previous post, I extracted - when available - the place and country of birth.
The result is this simple interactive website, showing how deeply transnational the making of international teams is now, since rugby became a professional sport in the late 1990s.
You can explore the composition of each team or identify which country is the most represented across the 20 teams.
The script and the data can be accessed in this repository.

title: 117 years of French rugby on a map
Julien Migozzi
text:
ENGLISH FRENCH


Research
At the intersection of economic geography, urban studies, and economic sociology, my research investigates how digital capitalism reshapes markets, cities, and inequalities, combining ethnographic fieldwork with spatial analysis and computational social science.
My primary focus examines how digital platforms and financial capital transform property markets, renewing inequalities of class, race, and wealth. South Africa is the central site of this work to analyse how tech-driven markets intersect with and affect long-standing patterns of social stratification and urban segregation.
The geography of finance is my second line of research, exploring how digitization reshapes financial markets, centres, and practices, with an emphasis on FinTech, venture capital, and retail investors. I am a co-author of the Atlas of Finance, published at Yale University Press in 2024.


Background
Before joining Cambridge, I was an Urban Studies Foundation Postdoctoral Research Fellowship and a Research Associate in Finance and Geography at the University of Oxford. Previously, I served as a Lecturer in the Department of Geography at the École Normale Supérieure (2017-2021).
I held visiting positions at the African Centre for Cities at the University of Cape Town (2016, 2023) and at the Department of Sociology at UC Berkeley (2024-2025).
I am an early-career representative at the Global Network on Financial Geography.
Most of my education took place in France, graduating from the École Normale Supérieure de Lyon as an élève normalien (student fellow). I earned a Master’s degree in Geography and Social Sciences and passed the agrégation. During my studies, I spent time abroad as a visiting student at Victoria University of Wellington.
I completed my PhD at the University of Grenoble Alpes and at the research centre Géographie-cités in Paris, where I remain a Research Associate. My PhD dissertation received two national awards: the Doctoral Prize in Urban Studies and the Best Dissertation Award in Geography.
I have written - and continue to write - about rugby, a passion that led me into the world of social sciences and fieldwork, and I keep a geographical and sociological eye on the sport’s transformation into a global industry.

title: Julien Migozzi
text:
I am a coauthor of the Atlas of Finance.
The Atlas was published at Yale University Press in 2024 and translated into 22 languages.
Our team was led by Professor Dariusz Wójcik (NUS), and included Takis Iliopoulos, Stefanos Ioannou, Liam Keenan, Timothy Monteath, Vladimir Pažitka, Morag Torrance and Michael Urban, in collaboration with Oliver Uberti and Professor James Cheshire (UCL).

Awards

Best Book of the Year in Economics, Professional and Scholarly Excellence (PROSE) Awards, Association of American Publishers, 2025
Best Single Volume, Professional and Scholarly Excellence (PROSE) Awards, Association of American Publishers, 2025
Gold Prize, Axiom Business Book Award, 2025
Best Design, Association of University Presses Design, 2025
British Cartographic Society Award, 2025
The Collins Bartholomew Award for thematic mapping & infographics, 2025
Stanfords Award for printed mapping
Winner of the popular vote in the atlas category at the International Cartographic Exhibition, Vancouver 2025.
Notable Contribution Award by the Geographical Association



Reviews

This volume gives readers a real, intrinsic view of how, where, and when financial markets do their work. It follows the history of money from cuneiform tablets to Bitcoins, showing how the essence of finance is in the networks that it both creates and destroys; and each page bursts with stunning and illuminating illustrations. Everyone from students to seasoned professionals to armchair experts will learn something from this unique and fun book.
— Robert J. Shiller, Nobel laureate in Economic Sciences


Erudite, spectacular, and revealing, the Atlas of Finance sheds a bright light on the history and geography of finance — its contribution to unleashing human potential but also its darker side. A tremendous accomplishment!
— Gabriel Zucman, Paris School of Economics and UC Berkeley


The book is a lot of fun. Data-fixated readers will enjoy unpicking its main content: a series of elegantly produced maps, graphs and other visualisations. These are eclectic in scope and ambitious in intent.
— Financial Times

The Atlas was reviewed in the Financial Times, Nature, the Irish Times, and the Wall Street Journal.

title: Digital capitalism,cities & inequality
text:
Economic Geographer · University of Cambridge
    Digital capitalism,cities & inequality
    
      I am an Assistant Professor in Development Studies in the Department of Politics and International Studies
      at the University of Cambridge.My research explores how digital capitalism reshapes property, finance, and inequalities — with a focus on South Africa.

title: Julien Migozzi
text:
University of Cambridge
At Cambridge, I teach a class on “Digital capitalism” for the MPhil in Development Studies.


University of Oxford (2021 - 2025)
I regularly give guest lectures for the MSc in Sustainable Urban Development.


Ecole Normale Supérieure, Paris (2017–2021)

Economic and urban geography — graduate level
Mapping and spatial analysis with R — graduate level, Master in Quantitative Social Sciences
Statistics and mapping with R — undergraduate level
Introduction to urban fieldwork — undergraduate and graduate level
Masterclass “Digital Technologies in the African city” — graduate level
A 10-day graduate masterclass in partnership with the Ecole Normale Supérieure and the African Center for Cities, with participants from Nigeria, France and South Africa. The program featured guest speakers, collective workshops, site visits, and participant-led research projects.
Masterclass “Researching urban public spaces” — graduate level
A 10-day graduate masterclass organized by the Ecole Normale Supérieure (France), the Universities of Lagos (Nigeria) and the French Institute for Research in Africa (Nigeria). The program featured guest speakers, collective workshops, site visits, and participant-led research projects.



University Grenoble Alpes (2014–2017)

Urban geographies of globalisation — undergraduate level
Mainstream policies and grassroot initiatives for local development — graduate level
Geographic Information Systems — undergraduate level
